In a speech of Plenipotentiary Von Ribbentrop before the League of Nations on 19 March 1936, this opinion was expressed in the following terms -- I quote from Document Book 1, Page 32...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Stahmer, I have before me now the order of the Tribunal of 26 February 1946, and Paragraph 4 of that order is in the following terms: "The following documents are denied as irrelevant," and then the heading "Goering," and the fourth of the documents is the speech by Paul Boncour on 8 April 1927; and the sixth is the speech by Lloyd George on 7 November 1927, which you have not read but which you have put into your trial brief. I would again call your attention, and the attention of all the Defense Counsel, to the fact that they will not be allowed to read any document which has been denied by the Tribunal. Go on.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: This quotation is as follows: ". . . but it is also clear that if a world power such as France, by virtue of her sovereignty, can decide upon concluding military alliances of such vast proportions without having misgivings on account of existing treaties, another world power like Germany has at least the right to safeguard the protection of the entire Reich territory by re-establishing within her own borders the natural tights of a sovereign power which are granted all peoples." Before I take up the question of aggressive war in detail I have the intention, if I have the permission of the Tribunal, to call on the first witness, General of the Air Force Bodenschatz.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, certainly. [The witness Karl Bodenschatz took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What is your name?

turns.speaker: KARL BODENSCHATZ (Witness)
turns.text: Karl Bodenschatz.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: I swear by God -- the Almighty and Omniscient -- that I will speak the pure truth -- and will withhold and add nothing [The witness repeated the oath In German.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You may sit down if you wish.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: General Bodenschatz, since when have you known Reich Marshal Goering?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I have known Reich Marshal Goering since June 1918.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: In what capacity did you get to know him?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I came to know him when he was the commander of the Richthofen Squadron. I was at that time the adjutant of Rittmeister Freiherr von Richthofen who had just been killed in action.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were you taken into the Reichswahr at the end of the first World War?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: At the end of the first World War I was taken into the Reichswehr as a regular officer and remained from the year 1919 until April 1933.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: When, after the completion of the World War, did you resume your connection with Goering?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: In November 1918 I was with Goering at Aschaffenburg, at the demobilization of the Richthofen Fighter Squadron, and later in the spring of 1919 I was with him again for several weeks in Berlin. There our paths separated. Then I met Goering for the first time again at his first wedding, and I believe that was in the year 1919 or 1920. I cannot remember exactly. Up to 1929 there was no connection between us. In the year 1929, and until 1933, I met Hermann Goering several times here in Nuremberg where I was a company commander in Infantry Regiment 21. My meetings with Goering here in Nuremberg were solely for the purpose of keeping up the old friendship.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And then in the year 1939, you entered the Luftwaffe?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: In 1933 I reported to Hermann Goering in Berlin. At that time, Goering was Reich Commissioner of the Luftwaffe and I became his military adjutant.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How long did you retain this post as adjutant?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I retained this post as adjutant until the year 1938. Later I became Chief of the Ministerial Bureau, 1938.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And what position did you have during the war?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: During the war, I was liaison officer between the Commander-in-Chief of the Luftwaffe the Fuehrer's headquarters.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were you at the headquarters, or where?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I was alternately at the Fuehrer's headquarters and at the headquarters of the Commander-in-Chief of the Luftwaffe..

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: When did you leave that position?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I left that position on 20 July 1944, because I was seriously wounded that day.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And what was the cause of your being wounded?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The plot against Hitler.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: You were present?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And what were your tasks at the Fuehrer's headquarters?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: It was my duty in the Fuehrer's headquarters to report on special events, special matters, inquiries, and desires of the Reich Marshal if he were absent, and to transmit them. I also had to transmit inquiries from the Fuehrer's headquarters direct to Hermann Goering. Then I had to inform Hermann Goering early, that is, not through of ficial channels, regarding all that took place in the Fuehrer's headquarters insofar as it was of interest to him in his capacity as Reich Marshal.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you take part regularly in the conferences?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I was a listener at these conferences.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: From what time onwards did Reich Marshal Goering lose his influence with Hitler?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: According to my personal opinion and conviction, Hermann Goering began to lose influence with Hitler in the spring of 1943.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And what were the reasons?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: That was the beginning of large-scale air attacks by night by the R.A.F. on German towns, and from that moment there were differences of opinion between Hitler and Goering which became more serious as time went on. Even though Goering made tremendous efforts, he could not recapture his influence with the Fuehrer to the same extent as before. The outward symptoms of this waning influence were the following: First, the Fuehrer criticized Goering most severely. Secondly, the eternal conversations between Adolf Hitler and Hermann Goering became shorter, less frequent, and finally ceased altogether. Thirdly, as far as important conferences were concerned, the Reich Marshal was not called in. Fourthly, during the last months and weeks the tension between Adolf Hitler and Hermann Goering increased to such a degree that he was finally arrested.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know anything about this arrest? What was the cause?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I have no exact information about it. I can only tell you what I heard. I was at that time in Bad Reichenhall in the military hospital. I merely heard that Reich Marshal Goering had sent a telegram to the Fuehrer, and in this telegram Goering requested that, since the Fuehrer no longer had freedom of action, he might act himself. As the result of this telegram, which was sent by wireless to Berlin, the arrest took place. I would like to emphasize that I only heard that. I have no proof of any of these statements.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And who made the arrest?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot tell you about that because I know nothing. I heard, however, that a Kommando of the SS from Obersalzberg made the arrest.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did Field Marshal Goering have any previous knowledge of the incidents against the Jews which took place during the night of 9 to 10 November 1938?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Goering had no previous knowledge of these incidents. I inferred that from his demeanor -- how he acted towards me with regard to these incidents. He acted in the following manner: When he heard of these happenings he was dismayed and condemned them. A few days later he went with proof to the Fuehrer and complained about the people who had instigated these incidents. Captain Wiedemann, the adjutant of the Fuehrer, can give you further particulars on the subject on oath. Several weeks later, Hermann Goering called all the Gauleiter to Berlin, in order to make clear his attitude regarding the incidents of the 9th and 10th. He was violently opposed to these individual acts of barbarism. He criticized them severely as unjust, as economically unreasonable and harmful to our prestige in foreign countries. The former Gauleiter, Dr. Uiberreither, who took part in this conference of Gauleiter, has already given further particulars on oath.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were you present at a conference which took place in the beginning of August 1939 at Soenke Nissen Koog near Husum?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes. I personally took part in that conference.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Who was present there?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: As far as I remember the following were present: Hermann Goering; Herr Dahlerus, from Stockholm; six to eight English economic experts, whose names I do not recall; I was present, and there was an interpreter, Ministerialrat Dr. Bocker.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Can you tell us about the subject of this conference?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot remember it word for word, but as far as I can tell you Hermann Goering made the following statements . . .

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Stahmer, did the witness say where this conference took place?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Would you tell us where it was?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: [To the witness.] Please repeat where this conference took place.

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The conference took place at the beginning of August at Soenke Nissen Koog near Husum, Schleswig-Holstein.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Please continue. You were going to tell us about the subject of this conference.

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I repeat, in substance, Goering made the following statement: At that moment relations between England and Germany were very tense. Under no circumstances should this tension be increased or peace be endangered. The welfare and the trade of our two countries could only flourish and prosper in peace. It was to the greatest interest of Germany and Europe that the British Empire should continue to exist. Goering emphasized that he himself would do his utmost for the maintenance of peace. He requested the British business leaders, on their return home, to use their influence in authoritative circles for that purpose.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did Goering give you his opinion on how the foreign policy of the Reich should be carried out? When and on what occasions did conversations take place?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Hermann Goering often discussed these topics with me, in 1938 and 1939, especially during the period following the Munich agreement. These conversations would take place perhaps in connection with a report, or perhaps in his special train. Hermann Goering was always of the opinion that the policy of the Reich must be directed in such a way as to avoid war if possible. Hermann Goering dealt with this topic at particularly great length in a conference with the Gauleiter in the summer of 1938 in Karinhall. Dr. Uiberreither, whom I have previously mentioned, has already given further sworn testimony to this effect.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did Field Marshal Goering speak to you before leaving for Munich in September 1938?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Before Hermann Goering left for Munich, he told me he would do everything within his power to effect a peaceful settlement. He said, "We cannot have war." He exerted his influence on the Fuehrer to this effect, and during the negotiations in Munich he worked decisively for the preservation of peace. When he left the conference hall after the conference at Munich he said to us spontaneously, "That means peace."

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did he often discuss with you for what reason he was against a war, and on what occasions?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: We talked about this topic very frequently. He always said to me: "In the first World War as an infantry officer and as an air force officer I was constantly at the front. I know the horrors of a war, and, therefore, my attitude is to preserve the German people from these horrors if possible. My ambition is to solve conflicts peacefully." In general, his opinion was that war is always a risky and unsure business. Even if you win a war, the advantages are in no relation whatsoever to the disadvantages and sacrifices which have to be made. If you lose the war, then, in our position, everything is lost. Our generation has already experienced the horrors of a great World War and its bitter consequences. To expect the same generation to live through another war would be unthinkable. I would like to add that Hermann Goering, according to his inner thoughts and character, was never in favor of war. Nothing was further from his mind than the thought of a war.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did Goering converse with you about what were, according to his wish, the aims to be accomplished by the rearmament which Germany had undertaken? When and on what occasion?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Hermann Goering spoke with me about these matters in the year 1935 after the Wehrfreiheit had been proclaimed. He described Germany's rearmament, after vain attempts to achieve general limitation of armament, as an attempt at equality with the armament of other countries, in order to be able to collaborate with other powers in world politics with equal rights.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did conversations of this kind take place after 1935 also?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes. Now and then we resumed such conversations and he spoke in a similar vein.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you find out through Reich Marshal Goering what purpose the Four Year Plan was to serve?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I happened to speak with Goering about this matter in the year 1936, and that was the Four Year Plan had been announced. He explained it to me as follows: That in this plan he saw a means of securing for Germany those raw materials which she could not import in peacetime because of the lack of foreign exchange or whose import in an emergency might possibly be cut off.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: When and on what occasion did Goering give you his opinion on the Russian campaign?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Towards the end of 1941, after the first reverses in the Russian campaign, Hermann Goering talked with me about the fighting in the East. He said to me: "Adolf Hitler foresaw a very hard battle in the East, but he did not count on such reverses. Before the beginning of this campaign I tried in vain to dissuade Adolf Hitler from his plan of attacking Russia. I reminded him that he himself, in his book Mein Kampf , was opposed to a war on two fronts and, in addition, I pointed out that the main forces of the German Luftwaffe would be occupied in the East, and England, whose air industry was hit, would breathe again and be able to recover."

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Would that be a convenient time to break off for 10 minutes? [A recess was taken.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal has observed that the witness is using notes whilst giving his evidence. The ruling which I announced this morning was confined to the defendants and did not extend to witnesses. Nevertheless, the Tribunal will allow the same rule to be applied to witnesses. But the evidence must not be read, the purpose of the rule being merely to assist recollection in giving evidence. [Turning to Dr. Stahmer.] Yes, Dr. Stahmer.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know whether people turned to the Reich Marshal with the request that their relatives should be freed from concentration camps or to help them in their difficulties with the Gestapo?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The Chief of Staff is the person who can answer that question. I myself only heard that such requests were made to the Reich Marshal.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you not have to deal with such requests in the military section?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: In the military section I had to deal with the requests which were concerned with the Luftwaffe. But they were only requests regarding the arrests of German citizens who stated that they had not been given the reason for their arrest. We also received communications regarding detention, grievances, and also regarding arrests of Jews. Requests of this kind came to me only from Luftwaffe sources or from my immediate circle of acquaintances.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How were such requests treated?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Such requests were always treated as follows: Most of the requests, which came from the broad masses of the people, were submitted to the Reich Marshal through the Staff. Those requests that came from the Luftwaffe were presented through my office, and requests that came from the Reich Marshal's relatives or friends, they themselves presented. The Reich Marshal did not refuse his help in these cases. In individual cases he asked the Fuehrer personally for a decision. In all the cases that I dealt with help could be given.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did many Jews turn to Goering with requests for help?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, Jews, and particularly Jews of mixed blood applied to Reich Marshal Goering.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How were these requests handled?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The Reich Marshal did not deny his help and he gave instructions whenever possible that help should be given.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What was Goering's general attitude to human society?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: In his feelings, thoughts, and actions, as far as human society was concerned, he was a benefactor to all in need. He was always ready to help those who were in need, for instance sick people, wounded, the relatives of those who had been killed in the war and of prisoners of war. Care for the working classes was particularly important to him. Here is an example of this: The introduction of miners' compensation. Every miner who had completed 25 years of steady work was to receive over 20,000 marks. This is one of his most important social works.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you know of the conditions in the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I had no knowledge of the conditions in the concentration camps.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were the concentration camps spoken of at the Fiihrer's headquarters during discussions with the Fuehrer, or on any other occasion?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: In the Fuehrer's headquarters I never heard the Fuehrer speak about the concentration camps. He never discussed them in our circle.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Was the question of the annihilation of the Jews discussed there?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, it was not; not in his discussions with me, at any rate.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Not even in discussions on the war situation?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, I cannot remember him ever discussing the annihilation of the Jews in my presence during discussions on the war situation.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did anyone else there mention anything?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Not Himmler?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: He never discussed the subject with Himmler. I have only heard since being in prison that Himmler's reply to people who spoke to him on this matter was, "What you have heard is not true; it is incorrect." I personally did not discuss this question with Himmler.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you know how many concentration camps there were?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Everyone knew that the camps existed, but I was not aware that so many existed. It was only after the war that I learned the names of Mauthausen and Buchenwald from the newspaper. I only know of the camp of Dachau because I happen to come from Bavaria.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you never hear of the atrocities either?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, I never heard of the atrocities. The very first time I heard was last year, when I reported to the Reich Marshal -- to be exact it was the middle of March 1945 -- when I reported my departure on sick leave. The Reich Marshal told me during lunch that very many Jews must have perished there and that we should have to pay dearly for it. That was the first time that I heard of crimes against the Jews.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I have no further questions. I can now turn the witness over to the other Defense Counsel and to the Prosecution.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does any Defense Counsel wish to ask any questions of this witness? DR. HANS LATERNSER (Counsel for the General Staff and High Command of the German Armed Forces): I have only a few questions to ask this witness. [Turning to the witness.] Witness, in your capacity as liaison officer of the Commander-in-Chief of the Luftwaffe at the Fuehrer's headquarters you took part, as you have already mentioned, in the. discussions on the war situation. Did you also take part in discussions on the war situation when front-line commanders were making their reports to Hitler?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I personally did not take part in such discussions. At two discussions, however, I was in the adjoining room, once when Field Marshal Von Kleist was there for a conference, and the second time was when the leader of the Crimea Army came to make a report after the evacuation of the Crimea. I was, as I said, not actually present at those conferences, but I heard, in the adjoining room, that there were some differences of opinion between Hitler and the commander in question as they were raising their voices. That is all I can say.

turns.speaker: DR.LATERNSER
turns.text: Did you hear enough to follow the trend of this discussion?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, I could not follow the trend nor the substance of these discussions.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: In that case I have no further questions.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does any other Defense Counsel wish to ask any questions? [There was no response.] Then does the Prosecution wish to ask any questions?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: May it please the Tribunal. [Turning to the witness.] You are at the present time a prisoner of war of the United States?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I beg your pardon. Could you please repeat the question. I did not understand it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You are at the present time a prisoner of war of the United States?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: At the present time I am a prisoner of war of the United States.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You have been interrogated on a number of occasions by representatives of the United States?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I was interrogated several times by representatives of the United States.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You have also had a number of consultations with Dr. Stahmer who has just examined you?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I have had several discussions with Dr. Stahmer who has just addressed questions to me.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Those questions were addressed to you some time ago and you prepared your answers in writing?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Those questions were submitted to me beforehand and I was able to prepare my answers.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Coming to the subject of the concentration camps and the activities of your department in releasing persons from them -- as I understand, a large number of applications came to the Goering office for release from concentration camps?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I stated before that the requests for release from concentration camps did not come to my department but to the Staff office. I received only the requests and complaints in which people begged for help because they had been arrested, among them Jews who were to be arrested.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And were those applications that did come to you numerous?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: My sector covered only the Luftwaffe.. There were perhaps 10 to 20 such applications.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And those applications were from persons who were threatened with imprisonment, or had been imprisoned, or both?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Partly from people who were threatened with arrest and partly from people who had already been arrested.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And in each case, as I understand you, you intervened to help them.

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: On the instructions of the Reich Marshal, I helped in all cases that were submitted to me.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And did you know of any other cases that came to the Staff in which help was not given to the imprisoned persons?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I do not know anything about that. I only heard from Dr. Gritzbach, Chief of Staff, that requests that came to him also were settled in a humane way.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, were the persons that you intervened for innocent of crime or were you helping out those who were guilty of crime?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Those I helped were innocent people.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So it came to your notice that innocent people were being put in concentration camps?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Could you please repeat that question.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: It came to your notice that innocent people then were being put in concentration camps?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Had not been put into concentration camps, but were destined for them.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I thought you said you intervened for some who had been arrested.

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes; they were not taken to concentration camps. I will give you a practical example. A comrade of mine, from the Richthofen Squadron, a Jew by the name of Luther, was arrested by the Gestapo, that is to say, he was not taken to a concentration camp, but first was simply arrested by the Gestapo. His lawyer informed me. I informed the Reich Marshal of this case, and the Reich Marshal instructed me to have this man freed from his temporary custody by the Gestapo in Hamburg. He was not yet in a concentration camp. So far as I know this case happened in 1943.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What was he charged with when he was arrested?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: He was arrested because he was a Jew, and he had been told that he had committed an offense against decency in that he had been with an Aryan woman in a hotel.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And did you make any inquiries as to whether the charge was true?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I did not have to make such inquiries because I had no difficulty in obtaining his release. When I called up, he was released and thereafter stayed under the protection of Hermann Goering.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Whom did you call up to get his release?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The chief of the Gestapo office in Hamburg. I do not know the name. I did not make the call myself but had it done by my assistant, Ministerialrat Dr. Bottger.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So that the Gestapo would release persons upon the request of Hermann Goering?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Not from Hermann Goering's office, but the Reich Marshal gave instructions that it should be carried out, and it was carried out.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I thought you said your assistant called up. Did Goering also call the Gestapo himself?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, he did not call himself, not in this case.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So that even though this man may have been guilty of the charge, if he belonged to the Luftwaffe he was released, on the word of the Reich Marshal?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: He was not a member of the Luftwaffe, he was a civilian. He had previously been one of our comrades in the Richthofen Squadron. He was not in the Wehrmacht during the war.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But your instructions were to release all persons who were Jews or who were from the Luftwaffe? Were those your instructions from Goering?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The Reich Marshal told me, again and again, that in such cases I should act humanely, and I did so in every case..

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: How did you find out that Jews were arrested against whom there were no charges?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: In one case, in the case of the two Ballin families in Munich, these were two elderly married couples, more than 70 years old. These two couples were to be arrested, and I was informed of this. I told the Reich Marshal about it, and he told me that these two couples should be taken to a foreign country. That was the case of the two Ballin couples who, in 1923, when Hermann Goering was seriously wounded in front of the Feldherrnhalle, and was taking refuge in a house, received him and gave him help. These two families were to be arrested.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: For what?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: They were to be arrested because there was a general order that Jews should be taken to collection camps.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you knew of that order?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I did not know of the order. It was only through these examples which were brought to my notice that it became clear to me that this evacuation was to take place. I had never read the order myself nor even heard of it, because I had nothing to do with it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: It came to your attention that Jews were being thrown into concentration camps merely because they were Jews?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: In this case I am not speaking of concentration camps, but it was ordered that people were to be brought to collection camps.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Not concentration camps, but special camps? Where were they going from there?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: That I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And where was this special camp that you speak of?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I do not know where they were to be taken. I was told they were to be taken away.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But neither you nor Goering had any suspicion that if they were taken to concentration camps any harm would come to them, did you?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I knew nothing about what took place in the concentration camps.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now did you not hear about the concentration camps, and was not the purpose of your saving these people from going to them, that the people who went there were mistreated?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I must reiterate that I freed people from their first arrest by the Gestapo that were not yet in the concentration camp.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What would the Gestapo take them into custody for, if not the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: What purpose the Gestapo was pursuing with these arrests I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But you intervened to save them from the Gestapo without even finding out whether the Gestapo had cause for arresting them?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: If the Gestapo arrested any one, then they must have had something against him.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But you made no inquiry into that, did you?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I have already said it was generally known that these people were taken to collection camps, not concentration camps. It was known -- many German people knew that they were to be taken away. They knew that the people were taken to work camps, and in these work camps they were put to work.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Forced labor?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: It was just ordinary work. I knew, for instance, that in Lodz the people worked in the textile industry.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And where were they kept while they were doing that work?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot say, for I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: They were in a camp, were they not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot tell you all that, for I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You would not know about that?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I have no idea.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What is the difference between a work camp and a concentration camp? You have drawn that distinction.

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: A work camp is a camp in which people were housed without their being in any way ill-treated.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And a concentration camp is where they are ill-treated? Is that your testimony?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes. I can only tell you that now because in the meantime I discovered it through the press and through my imprisonment. At that time I did not know it. I learned it from the newspapers. I was a prisoner of war in England for quite a while, and I read about it in the English press.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You spoke of collection camps, that many people knew they were being taken to collection camps to be taken away. Where were they being taken away?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I do not know where they went from there.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you ever inquire?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, I never inquired.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You were adjutant to the Number 2 man in Germany, were you not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you never ventured to ask him about the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, I did not speak to him on that subject.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The only instruction you had was to get everybody out that you could.

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Where a request or a complaint was made, I followed those cases down, and in those cases I assisted.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You knew that Hermann Goering was a close co-worker with Himmler, did you not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I did not know that he was a fellow worker with Himmler, because he never worked with him directly. Himmler frequently came for discussions with Hermann Goering, but these were private conversations just between the two.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you knew that he was not only a friend, but that he had aided Kaltenbrunner to his post when Kaltenbrunner came into office, did you not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, that I did not know.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You did not know that?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I did not know that Reich Marshal Goering recommended Kaltenbrunner for his office. My activity was confined simply to the military sector. I was military adjutant to the Reich Marshal. I had nothing to do with these matters.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you have anything to do with the procedure of making full Aryans out of half-Jews?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: On the question of mixed blood, requests concerning the Luftwaffe came to me, and in fact, officers, according to the regulations, would have to be dismissed if they were of mixed blood. In many cases the Reich Marshal gave instructions that these officers should not be dismissed.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What was done about it?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: In these cases the chief of the personnel office was instructed not to dismiss these officers.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And in some cases some kind of an order was made, was it not, that they were full Aryans, notwithstanding Jewish parentage?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: At the moment I can remember no such case.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You spoke of the requests for help from Goering coming from broad masses of the people, and those requests were submitted to his staff. Is that right?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And who was the head of that staff?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: At the head of that staff stood the Chief of Staff, Dr. Gritzbach.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: How many assistants did he have?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: There were three sections, a press section, with Dr. Gerner in charge of that, and the private secretariat -- there were three sections.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And which of these sections handled the peoples' requests for relief from arrest?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Dr. Gritzbach and Dr. Gerner were concerned with that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: To whom did they talk about these matters, do you know?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: These gentlemen, as well as myself, submitted these matters to the Reich Marshal.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So that he was kept fully informed of what you did and of what they did?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Please repeat the question.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The Reich Marshal was kept fully informed of these applications to you and to the other sections?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: He was informed by me.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And, as I understand you, he never failed to give his assistance to any one of the applications that was made to him, so far as you know?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: As regards requests addressed to my office or to me personally he never refused assistance and actually help was always given.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And never inquired into the guilt or innocence of the person he was helping?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: They were innocent; that was clearly established.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, you were present on the 20th of July at the bomb explosion, as I understand from your direct testimony?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: On 20 July I was present at that meeting and stood very near the bomb.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Where was Hermann Goering on that day?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Hermann Goering was in his headquarters on that day, about 70 kilometers from the Fuehrer's headquarters.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Only 70 kilometers away; is that right? And at what time were you instructed to represent him at that meeting?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I was not instructed to represent him at this meeting. I took part in this conference, as in any other, as a listener. I had no orders to represent Goering, to represent him in the Fuehrer's headquarters. I was merely in the Fuehrer's headquarters to inform him of what went on there.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You represented him to listen, but not to talk; is that right?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I did not say very much during those years. I was simply a listener and had to inform him as to what took place at the conference; what would interest him in his capacity as Reich Marshal.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: How far in advance of that meeting were you instructed to attend?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: At this meeting? On 20 July? On 19 July I was on a special commission, sent to the Munster Camp to take part in the review of an Italian division. On 20 July, at noon, I came by air to the Fuehrer's headquarters, gave Hitler a military communication, and Hitler said to me, "Come and discuss the situation." I did not want to go, but I went with him and after 15 minutes the attempted assassination took place.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who sent you with the message? Whose message was it that you were delivering?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I was commissioned at that time by Reich Marshal Goering to attend the review of the Italian division at the Munster Camp and to tell Field Marshal Graziani that the men in that division were to be used to command flak guns. After Field Marshal Graziani had declared himself in disagreement with this, I was obliged to go to the Fuehrer's headquarters by air. It had been proposed that I should go by Mussolini's special train which was in Munster, and on the night of 19 to 20...

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Answer my question, Witness. Just answer the question, please, and you will save us a great deal of time. Whose messages were you carrying to the Fuehrer?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I brought the message that Graziani was not disposed to hand over these soldiers of the Italians division.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And before you started for the Fuehrer's headquarters you communicated with Goering about it, did you not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Before my departure, when I flew to Munster Camp -- that was a few days before -- I spoke to him and when I returned, before reporting to the Fuehrer, I telephoned Hermann Goering in his headquarters and gave him the same message.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And did he instruct you to go to the Fuehrer's headquarters at that time and give the message to the Fuehrer?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: This trip from Munster Camp I made on my own initiative because it was important for Adolf Hitler to know of this information before Mussolini, who was expected to arrive at the Fuehrer's headquarters at 3 o'clock in the afternoon on 20 July....

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: As I understand you, Goering wanted a peaceful outcome of the negotiations at Munich?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: He said that to me several times.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And he was highly pleased with the outcome that was achieved there?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: He was very pleased. I emphasized that before when I said that when he came from the conference room, he said spontaneously, "That means peace."

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And when you say that Goering wanted peace with Poland, he also wanted that same kind of a peace, did he not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Regarding peace with Poland, I did not speak to him.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did he send someone or induce Hitler to take someone to Munich in order to countercheck Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: All I know personally on this subject is this: Here, in imprisonment, Captain Wiedemann told me that Hermann Goering had expressed the wish that Von Neurath should be taken, and Wiedemann told me that Hitler had granted that wish.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, you were interrogated by the United States about this subject before Wiedemann got here, were you not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Before?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Before Wiedemann was brought here.

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I was not interrogated on this subject -- the Munich Agreement and Von Neurath.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were you interrogated on the 6th of November 1945, and did you not then say that Goering used very harsh words about Ribbentrop and asked Hitler to take Neurath to Munich with him in order to have a representative present? Did you not say that to the interrogators of the United States?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot remember at the moment. If that is in the record then it must be so.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: This meeting as to which you have --  oh, by the way, after Munich you know that Goering gave his word of honor to the Czechs that there would be no further aggression against them, do you not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Please repeat the question.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You know that after Munich, when Goering was pleased with the outcome, he gave his word of honor that there would be no further aggression against the Czechs. Do you know that?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, I did not know that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: This meeting that took place in London, I mean the meeting that took place when the Englishmen were present . . .

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: In Husum, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who was the Swedish person who was present?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Herr Dahlerus was the Swede who was present.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who were the English who were present?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: There were six to eight English economic experts. The names I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And at that time -- by the way, have you fixed the time of that? What was the date?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot say precisely. It was the beginning of August.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Was it not 7 August?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot say.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Was Mr. Dahlerus there?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The question as to whether Dahlerus was there -- I cannot remember one hundred percent whether he was there. I know only that when I spoke to my lawyer he said that Dahlerus was there, but I cannot swear one hundred percent that he was there. I assumed he was, since the Defense Counsel Dr. Stahmer told me that he was there. That was the reason why I said previously that Hermann Goering and Dahlerus were present at that meeting.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And the subject under discussion was the Polish relations with the German Reich?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Polish relations were not discussed, but relations between England and Germany. There was no talk of relations with Poland.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Goering wanted the English gentlemen to see that England did not attack Germany?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: He did not express it quite that way. He said, as I have already stated, the English gentlemen should, when they returned home, work in the same way that he was working -- for peace, and to make their influence felt in important circles.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, was that not said in connection with the Polish negotiations that were then going on?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: With the Polish negotiations? I cannot remember that any mention was made of Polish negotiations.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were you with Hermann Goering when the Polish war broke out?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: When the Polish war broke out I was in Berlin.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were you still in your office under Hermann Goering's command?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, I was at that time under Hermann Goering's command.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: When did you first begin preparing for a movement of your forces in the direction of Poland?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot make any definite statement on that subject; that was a matter for the General Staff. I know only that during the period before the outbreak of war the Chief of the General Staff several times visited the Commander-in-Chief of the Air Force, Hermann Goering, and that such matters were discussed. I, myself, was not informed as to how many forces were to be used in the Polish campaign.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were you present at the conference in which Hermann Goering stated that he, right after Munich, had orders to multiply the Air Force by five?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot recall having been present at any such discussion.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You know that the Air Force was greatly enlarged after Munich?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, I do not know that. The Air Force was augmented according to plan. In this connection I can say for certain that the German Air Force, at the beginning of the Polish campaign, as regards leadership, planning, or material, was not equal to its task.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Mr. Justice Jackson, would you like to adjourn now or would you like to go on in order to finish?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: This would be a convenient time. I am sure we cannot finish before lunch hour.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You would like to adjourn now?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Yes, Sir.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well. [The Tribunal recessed until 1400 hours.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: We will have no open session tomorrow.

turns.speaker: GENERAL R. A. RUDENKO (Chief Prosecutor for the U. S. S. R.)
turns.text: I want to say a few words with respect to the statement of Defense Counsel Stahmer. When speaking about the document concerning the German atrocities at Katyn, Defense Counsel Stahmer stated that it was not in his possession. I do not want to speak about the nature of this document. I want to report to the Tribunal that on 13 February this document, as Exhibit USSR-54 -- 30 copies of it, all in the German language -- was given to the Document Room for the purposes of the Defense. We did not think that we had to present the document to each Defense Counsel separately. We considered that if the document were given to the Document Room, the Defense would take the necessary steps concerning it. That is all I wish to say on this matter.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: There must be a misunderstanding about the number of this document. It was submitted at that time in open session by the Russian Prosecutor as Exhibit Number USSR-64. USSR-64 has not been distributed. I have not received it, and upon request at Information Room of the Defense, upon two requests, I have not been able to obtain it.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Well, we will inquire into the matter. [The witness Bodenschatz took the stand.]

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Previous to the spring of 1943, as I understand you, Hermann Goering was a man of great influence in the councils of the Reich?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Before the year 1943 -- that is, until the year 1943 -- Hermann Goering always had access to the Fuehrer, and his influence was important.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: In fact, it was the most important in Germany outside of the Fuehrer himself, was it not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Within the Reich he had great influence, very great influence.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Air power was his special mission and his special pride, was it not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: As an old airman, he was very proud to be able to build up and lead the Air Force.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: He had more confidence in air power as a weapon of war than most of the other men of his time, did he not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: At any rate he was convinced that his Air Force was very good. But I have to repeat what I said before, that at the beginning of the war, in the year 1939, that stage had not been reached by the Air Force. I repeat that at that time the Air Force was, as far as leadership, training, and material were concerned, not ready for war.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But ever since you first went with Hermann Goering you had been rapidly building up the Air Force, had you not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The building up of the Air Force went relatively fast.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And when you first went with Goering  -- I have forgotten what year you said that was.

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I came to Hermann Goering in April 1933. At that time there was no Commander-in-Chief of the Air Force, but only a Reich Commissariat for Aviation. But even at that time, the beginning of the building up of the Air Force -- the first beginnings --  started. It was only after 1935, however, when freedom from armament restriction was declared, that it was speeded up.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And the building up of the Air Force was very largely in bombers, was it not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: It was not mainly bombers; it was mixed, both fighters and bombers.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Goering also had charge of the Four Year Plan?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: He was commissioned by the Fuehrer to carry out the Four Year Plan.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: He also held several other offices, did he not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Hermann Goering, besides being Commander-in-Chief of the Luftwaffe, was put in charge of the Four Year Plan. Before that, at the beginning of the seizure of power, he was Minister of the Interior and Prime Minister of Prussia, President of the Reichstag and Reichsforstmeister.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I notice that you use here, as you have used in your interrogations by the United States, the expression "seizure of power." That was the common expression used in your group, was it not, to describe the coming to power of Adolf Hitler?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: It cannot be used in this sense. At that time it was completely legal because the National Socialist Party was then the strongest party, and the strongest party nominated the Reich Chancellor, and the strongest party had, as such, the greatest influence. It must not be interpreted to mean that they usurped the power, but that they had the most influential and prominent position among the parties, that is, by the completely legal means of election.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You want to change the word "seizure"?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I have to change that. It is only an expression which was common usage in the press at that time.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Goering got along without any open break with Hitler until 1945, did he not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Until the year 1945 there was no open break. The arrest was only quite at the end, as I have said before.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But the arrest was the first open break that had occurred between them, was it not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, the first big break between the two which was apparent to the public. But since the year 1943, as I have said before, there was already a gradual estrangement in the attitude of the two men.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But that was kept from the public, was it not, kept from the German people?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: It was not so visible to the public. It was a development which took place gradually from the spring of 1943 to 1945 -- first to a small extent, and then the tension became greater and greater.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: When the arrest was made it was made by the SS, was it not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I only heard that. It was said that in Obersalzberg a unit of SS had arrived which arrested Hermann Goering in his small house and confined him there. As to that, perhaps the witness who is going to testify later, Colonel Brauchitsch, who was present at this arrest and who was arrested himself, can give more details.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You were not arrested by the SS?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: At that time . . . since 20 July 1944, when I was seriously injured, I had been in the hospital. I was close to Berchtesgaden, at Bad Reichenhall, convalescing.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Whenever there were conferences which you attended, was it not the custom, at the conclusion of Hitler's address to the group, for Goering as the ranking man present, to assure the Fuehrer on behalf of himself and his fellow officers of their support of his plans?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Of course I was not present at all conferences; I only took the part of listener. At these discussions, or shall we say conferences, in which I took part, it happened from time to time that the Reich Marshal made a remark at the end and gave assurance that the will of the Fuehrer would be carried out. But at the moment I cannot remember specifically any such conference.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You cannot remember any conference at which he did not do it either, can you?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes. It was not always done; on the contrary, he did not do it as a rule. In the Reichstag Hermann Goering always made a concluding speech, after a session had ended, expressing his confidence in Adolf Hitler.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And did he not do that at every meeting of officers at which the Fuehrer was present?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: May I ask you to repeat the question? I have not quite understood it. I beg you to excuse me, but I would like to mention that owing to my injury I have lost 60 percent of my hearing, and therefore I beg you to excuse me if I ask for repetitions. Please, repeat your question.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Quite all right, Sir. Do you know of any conference between Hitler and his High Command at which Goering did not close the meeting, as the ranking officer present, by making assurances of support to Hitler's plans?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Some of the conferences I attended were concluded by a declaration of that nature. There were, however, many conferences -- in fact most of the conferences -- when nothing further was said at the end. When the Fuehrer had finished his speech, the meeting was ended.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: In 1943, when Goering began to lose influence with Hitler, it was a very embarrassing time for Goering, was it not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Hermann Goering suffered from this fact. He often told me that he would suffer very much on that account.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: From the fact that the Fuehrer was losing confidence in him?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: What was that?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: He was suffering from the fact that the Fuehrer was losing confidence in him? Was that what was causing his suffering?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: That may have been part of the reason, but differences of opinion arose about the Luftwaffe.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, in the spring of 1943 it was apparent to you and apparent to him that the war was lost for Germany, was it not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot say that. The Reich Marshal did not tell me in 1943 that the war was lost, but that there were great difficulties, that it would become very dangerous; but that the war was definitely lost -- I cannot remember that the Reich Marshal at that time, in the spring of 1943, made a statement to me of that kind, or a similar one.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The Reich Marshal had given his assurance to the German people, had he not, that it would not be possible for them to be bombed, as Warsaw, Rotterdam, and other cities were bombed?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: As far as I know, he did not give the assurance in those words. Before the war, when our Air Force was growing -- I mean at the beginning of the war, when the great successes in Poland and in France were manifest -- he said to the German people that the Air Force would do its job and do everything to spare the country from heavy air raids. At the time that was justified. It was not clearly foreseen then that matters would develop differently later.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Then he had given his assurance to the German people, had he not, that the Luftwaffe would be able to keep enemy bombers away from Germany?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot remember that he gave an official assurance to the German people in the form of a decree or a big speech. At times it was said that the German Air Force, after the successes in Poland and France, was at its peak. I do not know of any official statement whereby it was made known to the German people.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: At all events, it became apparent in the spring of 1943 that any such assurance, if it had been given, was misleading? BODENSCHATZ In the year 1943 the conditions were entirely different, owing to the fact that the British and American Air Forces came into the picture in such large and overwhelming numbers.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And it was also true that the air defenses of Germany were proving entirely inadequate to cope with the situation; is that not a fact?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The air defense of Germany was very difficult, as the entire defense did not depend on the air crews alone, but it was also a radio-technical war, and in this radio-technical war, it must be admitted frankly, the enemy was essentially better than we were. Therefore it was not only a war in the air, but it was also a radio war.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: It had become apparent that Germany could not cope with it -- is that not a fact? -- by 1943.

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: In the year 1943 it was not yet a hundred percent clear. There were fluctuations, low and high points. Efforts were made to increase the fighter strength at the expense of the bombers. It was not one hundred percent obvious that the enemy air force could not be opposed successfully. That became obvious only after the middle of 1944.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The Fuehrer lost confidence in Goering as the bombing of German cities progressed, did he not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, indeed, from the moment the British Air Force started with their large-scale attacks on German cities, particularly when the first heavy British air attack on Cologne took place. From that moment it was obvious that differences of opinion, at first not too serious, were arising between the two men.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Hitler accused Goering, did he not, of misleading him as to the strength of the air defenses of Germany?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I do not know that the Fuehrer ever accused the Reich Marshal of any offense in this respect. Discussions between Adolf Hitler and the Reich Marshal were, in spite of all tension, always very moderate. The criticism is said to have become more vehement only later, in 1944 and the beginning of 1945. But I was not present, because I had been off duty since 20 July 1944.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I asked you a question. I did not intend to imply that the Fuehrer accused him of an intentional misstatement, but he had misled him or he had misunderstood the strength of Germany's air defenses. Was that not generally understood in your circle?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: There could be no question of misleading. The reports which the Air Force made to the Fuehrer were always correct. The weaknesses of the Air Force were also reported to the Fuehrer.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What were the efforts that were made by Goering, which you refer to as tremendous efforts, to recapture his influence with the Fuehrer?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The Reich Marshal, whenever there were conferences, asked through me that he might participate. The Reich Marshal came more frequently than usual to the Fuehrer's headquarters, and he also said to me, "I will try everything to regain the right contact with the Fuehrer." He said that personally to me.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And he was particularly careful after the spring of 1944 not to do anything that would offend the Fuehrer?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot say anything more about the year 1945, because then I was no longer active. I had no further contact.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, this bombing of German cities had become very troublesome from the point of view of the German people's criticism of the government, had it not, in 1944?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The German people suffered terribly under these bombing attacks, and I can only say one thing -- that Adolf Hitler suffered most from them. When at night the bombing of a German city was reported, he was really deeply moved, and likewise the Reich Marshal, because the horror of such a bombing was indescribable. I have experienced a few such bombings in Berlin myself, and whoever has lived through that, will never forget it as long as he lives.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And this was all becoming very embarrassing to Hitler and to the Reich Marshal, was it not, to explain to the German people why this was going on?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: That did not have to be explained, because the German people felt it. No explanation was given. It was only said that all possible measures would be taken to master this peril.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you knew at that time, and the Reich Marshal knew, that no measures could be taken that would prevent it?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: No, no, no. I emphasized before that it was a radio-technical war, and there were moments when, in the defense, we could counter the measures of the enemy while constantly discovering a new means to hit him.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: When you made the announcement to the German people that all means would be taken, you had then no means at your disposal, that you knew of, to use, did you, to prevent the bombing of the German cities?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Oh yes, indeed.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What were they, and why were they not used?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: There were, for example, the following means: The most important areas were protected by antiaircraft guns. Then there were radio-technical means, jamming transmitters, which would have made it possible, and which partly did make it possible, to jam the radio sets in the enemy aircraft.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The movement to satisfy the German people under the bombing attacks was a matter of great concern to the Reich Marshal, was it not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The Reich Marshal was very anxious that the population should be informed.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And see that the population was satisfied, was he not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: It is easy to say "satisfied." He could only assure the German people that he would do everything in his power to master these attacks.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, have you seen the Reich Marshal and Hitler when the reports came in of the bombing of Warsaw and Rotterdamand of Coventry?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot remember whether I was present when the reports came.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You never saw any such reactions on their part on those bombings, I take it?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I only know that Warsaw was a fortress which was held by the Polish Army in very great strength, provided with excellent pieces of artillery, that the forts were manned, and that two or three times Adolf Hitler announced that civilians should be evacuated from the city. That was rejected. Only the foreign embassies were evacuated, while an officer with a flag of truce entered. The Polish Army was in the city defending it stubbornly in a very dense circle of forts. The outer forts were very strongly manned, and from the inner town heavy artillery was firing towards the outskirts. The fortress of Warsaw was therefore attacked, and also by the Luftwaffe, but only after Hitler's ultimatum had been rejected.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Was Coventry a fortified city?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Coventry was no fortress. Coventry, however, was a city which housed the key industry of the enemy air force, in which the aircraft engines were built, a city in which, as far as I know, many factories were situated and many parts of these aircraft engines were manufactured. In any case, the Luftwaffe had at that time received orders to bomb only the industrial targets. If the city also suffered, it is understandable, considering the means of navigation at that time.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You were interrogated in November of 1945, were you not, by Colonel Williams?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, I was interrogated.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Colonel Williams asked you about certain fictitious incidents along the German-Polish border late in August of 1939, did he not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, he asked me about that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And would you care to tell the Tribunal what you know about the fictitious incidents along the Polish border?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I do not know anything positive. I was asked by Colonel Williams whether I knew in advance about the incident of the Gleiwitz broadcasting section. I told him I knew nothing about it. It was only that the incidents on the Polish border were very similar to those which happened on the Czech border. It may have been presumed -- that was only my opinion -- that they were perhaps deliberate. But I had no positive proof that anything had been staged on our part.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you tell him on the 6th of November 1945, as follows: "I heard about it, but I personally at that time had the feeling that all these provocations that had taken place had originated from our side, from the German side. As I said, I had no real proofs of that, but I always had that feeling." Did you not say that?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, I said that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And that you had talked with people about this, from whom you got that feeling. Is that right?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I cannot remember that very well now. I only know that the reports in the press gave me that suspicion.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You were asked, were you not, this question and gave this answer: "Question: But you are of the opinion that what appeared in the press and these incidents that were reported were not true, but done merely to cause an incident as an excuse for an invasion?" And did you not make this answer: "I had that feeling. I cannot prove it, but I definitely know I had a feeling that the whole thing was being engineered by us." Did you not make that answer to that question?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: The minutes will show it. If it is in the minutes, I said it. At the moment I cannot remember the exact words.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You do not deny the fact, however?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I had that feeling, but it was a purely subjective opinion.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But it was your opinion?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now then, I ask you whether you were not interrogated about the Fuehrer's desire to make war on Poland, and whether you did not give this answer: "Gentlemen, this question is very hard to answer, but I can state under my oath that the Fuehrer actually wanted the war against Poland. I can prove that he actually wanted a war of aggression against Poland by the circle surrounding the Fuehrer and the remarks that were made. I was present during the night when Hitler gave Henderson his conditions that he wanted Danzig, and I concluded from all the conferences that the Fuehrer had with the Ambassador -- I had the impression that the Fuehrer did not really want the Poles to accept those conditions." And I ask you if you made those answers to Colonel Williams?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: I can make the following answer to that: I was not present at the conference. If I said that, I did not express myself correctly. I was not at the conference that the Fuehrer had with Henderson, but I was standing in the anterooms with the other adjutants, and outside in the anteroom one could hear the various groups, some saying one thing, some another. From these conversations I gather that the conditions which Henderson received for the Poles in the evening were such, and that the time limit for answering these questions -- which was noon of the next day -- was so short, that one could conclude there was a certain intention behind it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, that is the impression that you received from being in the anteroom and talking with the people who were about Hitler that night?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: There were adjutants, the Reich Press Chief, and the gentlemen who were waiting in the anteroom without taking part in the conference.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I will ask you, in order to make this very clear, one more question about your interrogation on that subject. Were you not asked this question: "Then we can summarize your testimony this morning by saying that you knew in 1938, several months before Germany attacked Poland, that Hitler fully intended to attack Poland and wage an aggressive war against her; is that right?" And did you not make this answer: "I can only say this with certainty that from the night when he told Henderson that he wanted Danzig and the Corridor, from that moment, I was sure Hitler intended to wage an aggressive war." Were you asked that question, and did you make that answer?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: If it is in the minutes, I said it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, if it were not in the minutes, it would still be your testimony now, would it not? It is a fact, is it not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: My definition is precisely this: From the handing over of Adolf Hitler's demands to Henderson and from the short time that Henderson was granted, I conclude that there was a certain intention. That is how I should like to define it precisely now.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I will ask that you be shown Document Number L-79, United States exhibit in evidence, Number USA-27. You have seen that before, witness?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: A copy of this document was shown to me by Colonel Williams, and I told him that I myself could not remember having been present. But if my name is on the minutes, then I was there.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But your name is on the document, is it not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Then I was there. I cannot remember the subject of this conference. I told Colonel Williams that that must have been discussed because Colonel Schmundt, whose handwriting I know -- I was shown a copy -- I told him that Colonel Schmundt was a man who was very conscientious in making his notes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That is all in his handwriting?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: That is it as I see it here.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And it is signed by Colonel Schmundt?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, it is signed by Colonel Schmundt -- Lieutenant Colonel Schmundt. The corrections are not in his handwriting.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But the body of the document is his handwriting?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, that is his own handwriting. I know it; yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And when you were asked about that by Colonel Williams, you took time to read it, and then you said, did you not: "I think that the thoughts are right as they are expressed here; these are the thoughts that the Fuehrer usually voiced to us in a small circle." You made that statement?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, I did say that, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you said: "I cannot remember whether these things were expressed on that day. However, it is possible that the thoughts which are put down here are the thoughts of Adolf Hitler." You said that to Colonel Williams, did you not?

turns.speaker: BODENSCHATZ
turns.text: Yes, I said that to Colonel Williams.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That is all I care to ask about that, Sir. I now ask to have shown to you the original exhibit, Document Number 798-PS, Exhibit USA-29 in evidence.
